Now I walk before God—
in the light that only the living can see.

57.1:
To the choirmaster. 'Do Not Destroy.' A Michtam of David, when he fled from Saul into the cave.
Be merciful—God, be merciful—
my whole self runs to you for shelter.
I hide in the shadow of your wings
until the disasters that are hunting me sweep past.
57.2:
I am crying out to the God who is most high above all—
the God who brings to completion everything he intends for me.
57.3:
He will reach down from heaven and rescue me.
He will shame the one who is crushing me.
Selah.
God dispatches his steadfast love.
God dispatches his faithfulness—both of them coming toward me.
57.4:
I am lying down in the middle of lions—
people who are all fire and fury,
whose teeth are spears and arrows,
whose tongues are blades.
57.5:
Rise up, God—
higher than the heavens.
Let your glory be spread across the whole earth.
57.6:
They stretched a net across my path.
My soul was crushed to the ground.
They dug a pit right in front of me—
and fell into it themselves.
Selah.
57.7:
My heart is set firm—God, my heart is set firm.
I will sing.
I will make music.
57.8:
Wake up, everything in me that can glorify God!
Wake up, harp! Wake up, lyre!
I will be up before the dawn—I will wake it.
57.9:
I will thank you in front of all peoples, Lord.
I will sing praise to you among the nations.
57.10:
Your steadfast love is so vast it reaches to the heavens.
Your faithfulness stretches to the clouds.
57.11:
Rise up, God—
higher than the heavens.
Let your glory be spread across the whole earth.

58.1:
To the choirmaster. 'Do Not Destroy.' A Michtam of David.
A question for those who hold power:
Do you actually deliver what is right?
Do you judge human beings with fairness?
58.2:
No—your hearts are busy designing evil.
Your hands distribute violence across the earth.
58.3:
The wicked are off track from the moment they're born—
strangers to what is right from their mother's womb,
going astray from the very start, speaking lies.
58.4:
Their poison—serpent-venom.
Like a deaf adder that has blocked its own ear—
58.5:
—it will not hear the snake-charmer's song,
no matter how cleverly he plays.
58.6:
God—knock out their teeth.
LORD—pull the fangs from these lion cubs.
Disarm them completely.
58.7:
Let them drain away like water running off into nothing.
When they draw their arrows, let the arrows go nowhere.
58.8:
Let them melt like a snail leaving its trail of slime.
Like a baby born dead who never opened its eyes to sunlight.
58.9:
Before your pots even feel the thornwood burning beneath them—
greenwood and dry wood alike—
he will sweep them away like a whirlwind.
58.10:
The righteous will look up and rejoice
when they see justice done at last.
They will step through the ruin of the wicked.
58.11:
And then everyone will say it:
'There is, after all, a reward for doing what is right.
There is, after all, a God who judges the earth.'

59.1:
To the choirmaster. 'Do Not Destroy.' A Michtam of David, when Saul sent men to watch his house to kill him.
God—rescue me from my enemies.
Lift me up out of reach of those who are rising against me.
59.2:
Deliver me from evil workers.
Save me from violent men who want me dead.
59.3:
Look—they're crouched in ambush, waiting to take my life.
Powerful men have assembled against me.
And I haven't done anything wrong, LORD.
No transgression. No sin.
59.4:
Without any fault of mine they are up and running—ready to strike.
Wake up and come, LORD.
Look at what is happening here.
59.5:
You, LORD of heavenly armies—you are Israel's God.
Wake up and deal with all the nations.
Show no mercy to any of those who plot evil in treachery.
Selah.
59.6:
Every evening they come back—
howling like dogs,
circling the city.
59.7:
Their mouths spew poison—words like drawn swords on their lips.
And they think no one is listening:
'Who will hear us?'
59.8:
But you, LORD—you are laughing at them.
All the nations—you hold them in contempt.
59.9:
You are my Strength—I will keep watch for you.
God, you are the fortress I live in.
59.10:
The God of steadfast love will come out to meet me.
God himself will let me see my enemies brought low.
59.11:
Don't destroy them all at once—
my people might forget what they witnessed.
Make them stagger.
Bring them down in full view.
Lord, you are our shield.
59.12:
Let their words be the trap that catches them.
Their pride—the very pride in their mouths—let it be what brings them down.
For the cursing.
For the lies.
59.13:
Consume them in your anger.
Consume them until nothing remains.
Let the whole world see:
God rules in Jacob.
His authority reaches to the ends of the earth.
Selah.
59.14:
Evening comes around again.
They come back like dogs, howling.
Circling the city.
59.15:
They prowl for food.
If they don't find enough, they snarl.
59.16:
But I will sing of your strength.
In the morning I will sing aloud of your steadfast love.
You have been my fortress, my shelter—
right there in the day I needed it most.
59.17:
My Strength—I will sing to you.
God, you are my fortress.
You are the God who pours steadfast love over me.

60.1:
To the choirmaster. 'Lily of the Testimony.' A teaching Michtam of David, from the time he fought Aram-naharaim and Aram-zobah, while Joab was south striking twelve thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt.
O God—you have turned away from us.
You broke us.
You were angry.
Now come back—restore us.
60.2:
You shook the ground beneath us.
You split the land apart.
Repair the fractures—it is breaking down.
60.3:
You have taken your people through hard, crushing things.
You handed us a cup of reeling wine—
we could not walk straight.
60.4:
You raised a flag for those who fear you—
a rallying point,
a place to run to when the arrows are flying.
Selah.
60.5:
Deliver those you love, God.
Save us with your own right hand.
Answer us.
60.6:
God has spoken from his sanctuary—I believe it:
'I will triumph and claim this land as mine.
I will divide Shechem.
I will apportion the Valley of Succoth.'
60.7:
'Gilead belongs to me. So does Manasseh.
Ephraim is the helmet on my head.
Judah is the rod of my rule.'
60.8:
'Moab is a washbasin—fit for washing feet.
I toss my sandal over Edom like a man claiming what is his.
Over Philistia I raise my victory shout.'
60.9:
But who will take me into the fortress?
Who will lead me into Edom?
60.10:
God—it is you who turned us away.
You stopped marching out with our armies.
60.11:
Give us real help against the enemy.
Human rescue amounts to nothing.
60.12:
Through God—we will act with power.
He will be the one who tramples our enemies down.

49.1:
To the choirmaster. Of the Sons of Korah. A Psalm.
Hear this—all nations, all peoples!
Every soul alive in this world: listen.
49.2:
The nobodies and the powerful.
The destitute and the wealthy.
All of you.
49.3:
What comes from my mouth will be wisdom.
What rises from my heart will be understanding.
49.4:
I lean my ear toward a parable—a dark riddle—
and open its meaning with the harp.
49.5:
Why should I be afraid when evil days come,
when enemies who would trip me up press in on every side?
49.6:
What can people who boast in their money do to me?
Those who crow about the size of their fortune?
49.7:
Not one of them can ransom even their closest friend.
No one can buy a substitute before God—
49.8:
the price of a life is so far beyond reach
it can never be paid. Not ever.
49.9:
No price can buy a person immortality
or keep them from going down to Sheol.
49.10:
Look around—the wise die.
The fool and the dull-witted—all the same, all of them
leaving their wealth behind for strangers.
49.11:
Deep down they believed it: 'My house will stand forever;
my estate will go on for every generation.'
So they put their name on the land.
But the land has outlasted them all.
49.12:
Honor doesn't last.
Human splendor doesn't last.
A person in all their glory—
is no different from a beast that perishes.
49.13:
This is where self-trust leads.
And still—generation after generation comes along and admires them,
clapping for the same foolishness.
Selah.
49.14:
They are sheep—and Sheol is where they are headed.
Death itself is their shepherd now.
But the upright will have the last word when morning comes—
when the new day dawns, dominion belongs to them.
As for the rich: their form rots in Sheol,
far from every great house they built.
49.15:
But God—God will ransom me.
He will reach into Sheol and take me.
[The verb 'receive/take' — laqach — is the word used of Enoch: 'God took him' (Gen 5:24). Of Elijah (2 Kgs 2:3). The psalmist plants himself in that tradition.]
Selah.
49.16:
Don't let it unsettle you when someone gets rich,
when their household swells into an empire.
49.17:
They take nothing when they die—
not a coin, not an inch of reputation.
None of it follows them down.
49.18:
They congratulated themselves their whole life.
'You've done well!'—everyone said so.
People always praise the prosperous.
49.19:
But they go down to join their ancestors in the dark.
They will never see light again.
49.20:
Human splendor without wisdom—
all the wealth and honor in the world
without understanding that this is where it ends—
is no different from an animal that perishes.

50.1:
A Psalm of Asaph.
The Mighty One—God—the LORD—has spoken.
He summons the whole earth,
from the place the sun rises to where it sets.
50.2:
From Zion—beautiful, perfect Zion—
God blazes forth.
50.4:
He calls heaven and earth as witnesses.
He is bringing his people to court.
50.5:
'Bring my faithful people before me—
the ones who bound themselves to me with sacrifice.'
50.6:
The heavens themselves declare his righteousness.
God is the judge here—no one else.
Selah.
50.7:
'Listen, my people—I have something to say.
Israel—I am bringing my case against you.
I am God. Your God.'
50.8:
'I am not calling you to account over your sacrifices—
you have been bringing burnt offerings faithfully enough.'
50.9:
'I don't want a bull from your barns
or a goat from your flocks.'
50.10:
'Every animal in the forest already belongs to me.
The cattle on a thousand hills—mine.'
50.11:
'I know every bird that nests in the mountains.
Everything that stirs in the open field—mine.'
50.12:
'If I were hungry—which I am not—I would not come to you.
The world is mine. Everything in it.'
50.13:
'Do you think I eat bull meat?
That I drink the blood of goats?'
50.14:
'What I want is your thanksgiving—offered as sacrifice.
Keep the vows you made to the Most High.'
50.15:
'When trouble comes—call out to me.
I will deliver you.
And that deliverance will be your praise.'
50.16:
But to the wicked, God says this:
'What business do you have reciting my laws?
How dare you take my covenant on your lips?'
50.17:
'You hate being corrected.
You toss my words over your shoulder.'
50.18:
'You see a thief and fall right in with him.
You are comfortable with adulterers.'
50.19:
'Your mouth runs loose—straight into evil.
Your tongue is a craftsman of deception.'
50.20:
'You sit there tearing your own brother apart.
You slander the son of your mother's womb.'
50.21:
'You did all this, and I kept quiet.
You took my silence as approval—
assumed I was made of the same stuff as you.

But now I am speaking.
Now I set the charge down before you, point by point.'
50.22:
'Think carefully, you who have forgotten God—
before I tear you apart
and there is no one left to save you.'
50.23:
'The person who brings me thanksgiving—that person honors me.
The one who lays out a right way of living before me—
I will show that person the rescue that only God can give.'

51.1:
To the choirmaster. Of David. After Nathan the prophet came to him—after what happened with Bathsheba.
God—have mercy on me.
In your steadfast love—your relentless, covenantal love—have mercy.
In your great compassion—your womb-tenderness—
wipe away everything I have done wrong.
51.2:
Scrub me—work the stain all the way out.
Cleanse me from my sin.
51.3:
I know what I have done.
My sin is there—in front of me—every waking moment.
51.4:
Against you—and only you—have I sinned.
What I did was evil in your sight—I know it.
And because of that, whatever judgment you pronounce is right.
You are blameless in all of it.
51.5:
I was born into this.
Iniquity was there at my beginning—
sin was present at the moment of conception.
I am not making excuses—I am naming the depth of the problem.
51.6:
What you want is truth all the way down—
in the parts no one else can see.
And in that hidden depth, you are teaching me what wisdom actually is.
51.7:
Take the hyssop—the purification branch of Leviticus and the Passover—and cleanse me.
And I will be clean.
Wash me, and I will be whiter than snow.
51.8:
Let me hear joy again—let me hear gladness.
These bones you have broken—
let even them rejoice.
51.9:
Turn your face from my sins.
Blot out every last piece of my guilt.
51.10:
Create in me a clean heart, God.
[Bara — the Genesis 1 word for divine creation from nothing. Nothing less than new creation will fix what is wrong in me.]
Renew a right spirit deep inside me.
51.11:
Don't send me away from you.
Don't take your Holy Spirit from me—
the Spirit that is yours, that is holy—
the one gift I cannot live without.
51.12:
Give me back the joy of being saved by you.
Give me a spirit that is willing and open—
one that won't drag me back into this.
51.13:
Then—only then—I will be able to show other rebels the way back.
And sinners will actually turn and come home to you.
51.14:
Free me from the blood on my hands, God—
God, the God who saves me.
Then my tongue will break into song
about your righteousness—how right you are in all of this.
51.15:
Lord—open my lips.
I cannot even praise you properly right now.
You have to open the door yourself.
Then my mouth will declare your praise.
51.16:
You don't actually want animal sacrifices—
if you did, I would bring them by the cartload.
Burnt offerings don't move you.
51.17:
What you want is a broken spirit—
that is the sacrifice.
A heart shattered by the weight of what it has done.
A crushed and contrite heart, God—
you will never turn that away.
51.18:
Be good to Zion, LORD—in your grace and goodwill.
Build Jerusalem's walls.
[These final verses likely carry the community's prayer—David's penitence applied to the city's need, shaped by exile and the longing for restoration.]
51.19:
Then—and only then—will the sacrifices mean something.
The burnt offerings, the whole offerings—they will be real.
Bulls will go up on your altar, and it will matter,
because the heart behind them will be right.

52.1:
To the choirmaster. A Maskil of David. When Doeg the Edomite went to Saul and reported: 'David has come to the house of Ahimelech.'
O strong man—why boast about the evil you have done?
Doeg's power lasts a day.
God's steadfast love holds every day.
52.2:
Your tongue is a blade—
sharp, well-honed—
and you work it to cut and destroy.
52.3:
You prefer evil to goodness.
You prefer the lie to what is true.
Selah.
52.4:
Every word you love is a word that tears down.
You deceitful thing.
52.5:
But God will unmake you—completely, permanently.
He will snatch you right out of your comfortable life.
He will pull you up by the root
from the land of the living.
Selah.
52.6:
The righteous will watch what happens.
They will be struck with awe—
and then they will laugh:
52.7:
'Look at this man—
he would not make God his hiding place.
He trusted in money—mountains of it.
He made his home in his own capacity for harm.'
52.8:
But me—I am a green olive tree, rooted in God's house.
Alive. Bearing fruit.
I trust in his steadfast love—
not for a season. Forever.
52.9:
I will praise you forever—because of what you have done.
I will anchor my hope in your name.
Your name is good.
And I will do it all before your faithful people,
your gathered saints.

53.1:
To the choirmaster. On Mahalath. A Maskil of David.
The fool—the moral bankrupt—says in his heart: 'There is no God.'
Not a philosopher's conclusion; a lived conviction.
He lives as if there is no God to answer to.
They are corrupt. They do abominable things.
Not one does what is good.
53.2:
God looked down from heaven—
scanned across all of humanity—
to find anyone who actually understands,
anyone genuinely seeking him.
53.3:
Every one—turned away.
All of them together, rotted through.
Not one who does what is good.
Not one.
53.4:
Are these people completely without understanding—
those who consume my people like a meal,
who never once call out to God?
53.5:
Watch what happens:
there they are—seized by terror they never saw coming,
in a place they thought was safe.
God scatters the bones of the armies that moved against his people.
God shames them.
God has rejected them.
53.6:
Oh—if only the rescue of Israel would come out of Zion!
When God brings his people back from captivity—
let Jacob erupt in joy,
let Israel burst into gladness.

54.1:
To the choirmaster. With stringed instruments. A Maskil of David. When the Ziphites came to Saul and said: 'Isn't David hiding here among us?'
God—save me.
By your name—your very character and reputation—save me.
By your power, make my case.
54.2:
Hear my prayer, God.
Listen to what I am saying.
54.3:
People who owe me nothing have turned against me.
Brutal, ruthless men—hunting my life.
God is nowhere in their thinking.
Selah.
54.4:
But here is what I know:
God is my helper—my ezer.
[The same word as Eden: 'a helper suitable for him.' God himself takes that role.]
The Lord is the one holding my life up.
54.5:
He will turn the harm back on those who would harm me.
In your faithfulness, LORD—silence them.
54.6:
I will bring you an offering—freely, gladly, from an open hand.
I will give praise to your name, LORD.
Your name is good.
54.7:
He has rescued me from everything that threatened to undo me.
And my own eyes have seen my enemies undone.

61.2:
From the far ends of the earth I call out to you
when my heart is ready to give way —
lead me to the rock that towers above me.
61.3:
You have always been my shelter,
a tower of strength against the enemy.
61.4:
Let me dwell in your tent forever,
safe beneath the shelter of your wings. Selah.
61.6:
Add day upon day to the king's life;
let his years extend through generation after generation.
61.8:
So I will praise your name forever,
fulfilling my vows with every passing day.

62.2:
He alone is my rock and my rescue,
my stronghold —
I will not be greatly shaken.
62.3:
How long will you keep battering a man —
as if you mean to murder him?
You are nothing but a leaning wall,
a fence about to topple.
62.4:
Their whole aim is to topple him from his high place;
they love every lie.
With their mouths they speak blessing,
but inwardly they are cursing. Selah.
62.5:
Wait in silence for God alone, my soul —
he is the only source from which my hope can come.
62.6:
He alone is my rock and my rescue,
my stronghold —
I will not be shaken.
62.9:
Common people are nothing but a breath;
the powerful are a fraud.
Put them on the scales together —
they rise up lighter than vapor.
62.10:
Do not put your trust in extortion,
or find your identity in what you have stolen.
If wealth piles up,
do not give it your heart.
62.11:
God has spoken one truth
that I have heard confirmed again and again:
power belongs to God alone.
62.12:
And steadfast love belongs to you, O Lord —
for you repay every person
exactly as their deeds deserve.

63.1:
A Psalm of David, written when he was in the wilderness of Judah.

O God, you are my God —
earnestly I seek you.
My soul thirsts for you;
my flesh is faint with longing,
as if in a dry and waterless wilderness.
63.2:
So I look for you here
as I once gazed on you in the sanctuary —
beholding your power, your glory.
63.3:
Your steadfast love is better than life itself —
so my lips cannot hold back their praise.
63.4:
So I will bless you as long as I breathe;
I will lift my hands in praise of your name.
63.5:
My soul is satisfied as at a feast of the richest food;
my mouth overflows with praise upon joyful lips.
63.6:
In the quiet of my bed I remember you;
I think of you through all the watches of the night.
63.7:
You have always been my help —
so in the shadow of your wings I will shout for joy.
63.8:
My soul clings tightly to you;
your right hand holds me and will not let go.
63.9:
Those who are hunting my life to destroy it
will themselves go down
into the depths of the earth.
63.10:
They will fall to the sword;
they will become a meal for jackals.

64.2:
Hide me from the secret plots of the wicked,
from the noisy crowd of those who do evil.
64.4:
They shoot from ambush at the innocent,
attacking suddenly without hesitation or fear.
64.5:
They egg each other on with their evil scheme,
planning to lay hidden traps.
'Who will ever see us?' they say.
64.6:
They think up wickedness and say,
'We have the perfect plan!' —
but the depths of the human heart are beyond fathoming.
64.7:
Then God himself draws the bow —
his arrow flies and they are struck down suddenly.
64.8:
The very tongues they sharpened become their ruin;
all who witness their fate recoil in horror.
64.9:
Then all people will stand in awe,
proclaiming what God has done,
pondering the work of his hand.

65.2:
You who answer prayer —
to you all humanity will come.
65.3:
When our sins have become too heavy for us,
you are the one who covers them over.
65.4:
Blessed is the person you choose and draw close,
to live in the shelter of your courts.
We will feast on the goodness of your house,
the sacred bounty of your holy temple.
65.5:
With awe-inspiring acts of justice you answer us,
O God of our salvation —
the one hope of every corner of the earth
and of the most distant seas.
65.6:
With your own strength you planted the mountains where they stand;
you wrap yourself in power.
65.7:
You silence the roaring oceans,
the crash of wave upon wave —
and the uproar of nations as well.
65.8:
The people at the ends of the earth
stand in awe at your mighty signs;
you fill the morning and the evening
with shouts of joy.
65.9:
You come to water the earth
and drench it with abundance;
the river of God brims full.
You prepare the grain —
for this is how you have arranged it all.
65.11:
You crown the whole year with your goodness;
wherever you pass, abundance drips down.
65.12:
Even the wilderness meadows overflow with life;
the hills are wrapped in celebration.
65.13:
The pastures are dressed in flocks;
the valleys lie hidden under grain —
they shout their praise,
they break into song.

66.1:
To the worship leader. A Song. A Psalm.

Shout with joy to God, all the earth!
66.2:
Sing the splendor of his name;
give him glory in your praise.
66.3:
Say to God: 'Your deeds are terrifying in their power!
Your enemies cower before you
because of your overwhelming strength.'
66.5:
Come, look at what God has done —
how awe-inspiring his acts toward all of us.
66.8:
Bless our God, all you peoples;
let his praise ring out everywhere.
66.9:
He holds our lives safe from death
and keeps our feet from stumbling.
66.10:
For you have put us through the fire, O God;
you have refined us the way silver is refined.
66.11:
You let us be caught in the net;
you pressed the weight of suffering onto our backs.
66.13:
I will come into your house with burnt offerings;
I will pay every vow I made to you.
66.14:
Every word my lips poured out,
every promise my mouth made
in the dark days of my trouble.
66.15:
I will bring fattened animals as burnt offerings to you,
rams ascending in fragrant smoke —
bulls and goats as well. Selah.
66.16:
Come and listen, all you who fear God —
let me tell you what he has done for me.
66.17:
I called out to him with my mouth,
and praise for him was already rising on my tongue.
66.18:
If I had been nursing sin in my heart,
the Lord would not have listened.
66.19:
But God did hear —
he actually attended to the voice of my prayer.
66.20:
Blessed be God,
who did not turn away my prayer
or keep his steadfast love from me.

67.1:
To the choirmaster. With stringed instruments. A Psalm. A Song.
May God deal gently with us and give us his blessing.
May the light of his face fall on us.
Selah.
67.3:
Let the peoples praise you, God—
every last people on earth.
67.4:
Let the nations erupt in joy and song—
because you will govern them straight,
ruling every people on earth with justice.
Selah.
67.5:
Let the peoples praise you, God—
every last people on earth.
67.6:
The earth has opened and given what it held.
God—our own God—has blessed us.
67.7:
God blesses us.
And from the farthest edge of the world to the nearest,
people will stand in awe of him.

68.1:
To the choirmaster. A Psalm of David. A Song.
Up—let God rise!
Let his enemies scatter
and all who hate him run.
68.2:
Scatter them like smoke before a gust of wind.
Melt them like wax before a flame.
Let the wicked dissolve—right before God's face.
68.3:
The righteous, though—let them be overjoyed.
Let them celebrate before God.
Let the joy be extravagant.
68.4:
Sing to God! Sing praise to his name!
Exalt the one who rides the storm-clouds—
Yah, that is his name, the living God—
and come before him with your whole heart.
68.5:
In his holy home,
God is father to those who have no father,
defender of those whose husbands are gone.
68.6:
He places the lonely in families—gives them somewhere to belong.
He leads prisoners out into open country.
But those who refuse him are left in the wasteland.
68.7:
God—when you marched out ahead of your people,
when you cut through the wilderness at their head—
Selah—
68.8:
the earth shook.
The sky opened and rain poured down
in the presence of the God of Israel.
Even Sinai trembled before him.
68.9:
You sent soaking rain, God.
When your people were spent, you revived them—
made your inheritance strong again.
68.10:
Your people made their home there.
In your generosity, God, you provided
for those who had nothing.
68.11:
The Lord spoke his word
and a vast company of women carried the news—
Victory! Victory!
68.12:
The armies' kings scattered and ran—
and even those who had stayed behind at home
got to share in what was won.
68.13:
Though you lay in the dust and the ash—
you will shine like a dove's wings:
silver on every feather,
gold flashing in the light.
68.14:
When the Almighty routed kings across the land,
snow fell on Zalmon—
a white brilliance after the battle.
68.15:
O mountain of God—
you are like the heights of Bashan,
like that great, many-ridged peak.
68.16:
Why do you stare so jealously at this hill, you great mountains?
This is the one God chose—
this is where he decided to live.
The LORD will make it his home forever.
68.17:
God's war-chariots are past counting—
tens of thousands, and thousands more beyond that.
The Lord himself is present in their midst,
here in his holy place, as he was once at Sinai.
68.18:
You climbed to the heights, a procession of captives at your heel.
You took tribute from the nations—
even from those who had defied you—
so that the LORD God could set up his dwelling here.
68.19:
Praise the Lord—
he is the one who shoulders our load
every single day.
He is the God who saves.
Selah.
68.20:
This is our God—the God who saves.
And it is the LORD, our Lord, who holds the keys:
who gets out of death alive, and who does not.
68.21:
But God will split the skull of his enemies—
the proud, disheveled heads of those who keep on sinning
and refuse to turn.
68.22:
The Lord announced it:
'I will bring them back—from Bashan, from the deep sea.
There is no place too far or too dark
for me to retrieve what is mine.'
68.23:
—so that you can walk through the wreckage of your enemies,
and even the dogs that follow you
will lap up what remains.
68.24:
They have seen it—your processional, God.
My God, my King, entering his sanctuary
with all his glory.
68.25:
Singers at the front,
musicians following behind,
and in among them—
girls with tambourines.
68.26:
Bless God in the great gathering.
Praise the LORD, all you who spring from Israel's source.
68.27:
Little Benjamin out in front, leading—
and behind him the princes of Judah in their ranks,
the princes of Zebulun,
the princes of Naphtali.
68.28:
Your God decreed that you would be strong.
Now confirm it, God—finish what you started among us.
68.29:
Because of your house here in Jerusalem,
kings will come carrying tribute to you.
68.30:
Rebuke that beast crouching in the reeds—
the bull-herds among the nations' young—
until they throw down their silver and bow.
Scatter those who live for war.
68.31:
Egypt will send its own princes.
Ethiopia will rush to reach out toward God with open hands—
all of them coming.
68.32:
You kingdoms of the earth—sing to God!
Sing your praise to the Lord.
Selah.
68.33:
To the one who rides the oldest heavens—
he lifts his voice,
and it is a voice of power.
Hear it.
68.34:
Give God the glory of his strength.
His splendor is over Israel,
his power is in the skies above.
68.35:
Awe-striking is God from his holy place.
The God of Israel—he gives his people their strength,
makes them powerful.
Blessed be God.

69.1:
To the choirmaster. To the tune 'Lilies.' A Psalm of David.
Save me, God—
the water is at my throat.
69.2:
I'm sinking in deep mud—
no bottom, no foothold.
I've gone under into deep water,
and the current is overwhelming me.
69.3:
I have cried until I have nothing left.
My throat is raw.
My eyes are worn out from scanning the horizon for my God.
69.4:
The ones who hate me without reason—
there are more of them than hairs on my head.
My enemies who want to destroy me for things I never did—they are powerful.
And now I am being made to restore what I never stole.
69.5:
God, you know my failures—
the foolish things I've done.
My sins are completely visible to you.
69.6:
LORD God of hosts—
don't let those who trust you be put to shame because of what is happening to me.
God of Israel—
don't let those who seek you be brought to disgrace on my account.
69.7:
This is happening because of you—
I am carrying the reproach that belongs to you.
And it has covered my face with shame.
69.8:
My own brothers do not recognize me anymore.
To my mother's children, I am a stranger.
69.9:
I burned so completely for your house
that it consumed me from the inside.
Every insult aimed at you
has found its mark in me.
69.10:
I wept. I fasted—denied myself even food as a sign of grief.
And they turned it into something to mock me with.
69.11:
I wore sackcloth—
and became a joke.
A proverb for fools.
69.12:
The elders who sit at the gate whisper about me.
The drunkards have written songs about me.
69.13:
But I—I keep praying to you, LORD,
when the moment of your favor comes.
God, in all the breadth of your steadfast love—
answer me;
in the faithfulness of your salvation—
look at me.
69.14:
Pull me out of this mud—
do not let me go under.
Rescue me from those who hate me,
from these waters that are over my head.
69.15:
Do not let the flood current carry me away.
Do not let the deep swallow me.
Do not let the mouth of the pit close over me.
69.16:
Hear me, LORD—your steadfast love is good and real.
Turn toward me.
In all the depth of your compassion—turn.
69.17:
Do not look away from your servant.
I am in desperate trouble—
answer me, and do it now.
69.18:
Come close to my life and buy it back.
Set me free—
before my enemies finish what they started.
69.19:
You have seen it all—the reproach, the shame, the dishonor.
Every one of my adversaries is in your line of sight.
69.20:
The disgrace of it has broken my heart.
I am undone.
I looked for someone to feel sorry for me—no one.
I looked for someone who would sit with me in it—not one person.
69.21:
When I needed food, they gave me poison.
When I was parched, they handed me vinegar to drink.
69.22:
May the very table they feast at become a trap.
May what they take such comfort in
spring shut on them.
69.23:
Let their sight go dark—
blind to the truth.
Let their bodies shake and never be still.
69.24:
Pour your wrath over them.
Let your burning anger catch up with them and hold them.
69.25:
Let their homes stand empty.
Let no one move in where they lived.
69.26:
They hunted down the very one you had already struck.
They mocked the pain of those you wounded.
There is no mercy in them.
69.27:
Let their guilt accumulate—
more on top of more.
Never let them come into the place where you make things right.
69.28:
Erase their names from the scroll of the living.
Do not let them be counted among the righteous.
69.29:
But I—I am poor and I am hurting.
God, let your salvation be the thing that lifts me out of this.
69.30:
I will praise the name of God with a song.
I will make him great with thanksgiving.
69.31:
This—the song, the thanks—
pleases the LORD more than a prize bull,
more than any animal you could lead to an altar.
69.32:
Those who are poor and low will see this and be glad.
You who seek God—let it put life back in your heart.
69.33:
The LORD hears the poor.
He does not dismiss the ones who are imprisoned and cannot get out.
69.34:
Heaven and earth—praise him.
The seas and everything alive in them.
69.35:
God will save Zion.
He will rebuild the cities of Judah.
His people will live there—it will be theirs.
69.36:
The children of those who served him will inherit it.
All who love his name—
they will live there.

70.1:
To the choirmaster. A Psalm of David, to bring to remembrance.
O God—hurry!
Deliver me!
LORD—come quickly!
Help me!
70.2:
Let those who are hunting my life be covered in shame and confusion.
Let every one who delights in my destruction
be driven back in disgrace.
70.3:
Let those who mock me—'Aha! Aha!'—
be sent reeling by the very shame they dish out.
70.4:
Let everyone who is looking for you
burst out in joy and gladness.
Let those who love your salvation keep saying it:
'God is great!'
70.5:
Here I am—poor, needy, desperate.
O God—come quickly!
You are my help.
You are the one who delivers me.
LORD—don't wait.

71.1:
LORD, you are the place I run to.
Let me never be shamed.
71.2:
Deliver me by your righteousness.
Bring me to safety.
Bend your ear toward me—save me.
71.3:
Be the rock I can always run back to.
You have already issued the order to save me.
You are my rock.
You are my fortress.
71.4:
God—pull me out of the wicked man's grip,
out of the clutches of those who are unjust and violent.
71.5:
Lord GOD—you are the hope I have always had.
From my youth I have trusted no one but you.
71.6:
Before I was born I was already leaning on you.
You drew me out of my mother's womb.
You have been the subject of my praise every day since.
71.7:
To many my life has seemed like a wonder—a sign that God does something with what looks like nothing.
But you are the strong refuge where I have always been safe.
71.8:
My mouth is completely full of your praise.
All day long—your glory.
71.9:
Don't throw me away when I am old.
Don't leave me when my strength runs dry.
71.10:
My enemies are talking about me right now.
Those who are stalking me have put their heads together.
71.11:
'God has left him behind,' they say.
'Chase him down. Take him.
No one is coming to save him.'
71.12:
God—don't be far away.
God—hurry to help!
71.13:
Let my accusers be exposed and destroyed.
Wrap them in shame—
let scorn and disgrace be the only clothes they have.
71.14:
I choose to keep hoping—without stopping.
And I will keep adding to the praise I give you.
71.15:
All day long my mouth will be telling it—
your righteousness, your saving acts.
There are more of them than I can count.
71.16:
I will come in the might of the Lord GOD.
I will speak of your righteousness—and yours alone.
71.17:
God—you have been teaching me since I was young.
And all these years I have been telling people what you do.
71.18:
Even now—old, my hair turning gray—don't leave me yet, God.
Let me live long enough
to tell this generation what kind of strength you have,
to show every generation still to come what your power looks like.
71.19:
Your righteousness, O God, towers into the sky.
You have done great things.
Who is like you?
71.20:
You are the one who showed me every one of those troubles.
But you will bring me back to life.
From the very depths you will lift me up again.
71.21:
You will restore what I have lost—more than before.
You will surround me with comfort on every side.
71.22:
I will take up the lyre and praise you, my God—your faithfulness deserves it.
I will sing to you with the harp,
you Holy One of Israel.
71.23:
When I sing to you—
my lips will burst open with joy.
My whole redeemed self will be rejoicing too.
71.24:
My tongue will keep talking about your righteousness—all day long.
Because those who wanted to harm me?
They are the ones who ended up ashamed.

72.1:
A Psalm of Solomon.
God—give the king your own sense of justice.
Give the royal son your righteousness.
72.2:
May he govern your people rightly,
and bring real justice to the poor.
72.3:
May the mountains themselves carry peace down to the people.
May every hill overflow with it—through righteousness.
72.4:
May he take up the case of the poor.
May he deliver the children of those who have nothing.
May he crush whoever crushes them.
72.5:
May the fear of you last as long as the sun and moon—
through every generation, without end.
72.6:
May his reign come down like rain on fresh-cut grass,
like showers soaking into dry ground.
72.7:
May the righteous thrive in his day.
May peace multiply and overflow—
until there is no moon left to watch it.
72.8:
Let his rule stretch from sea to sea,
from the Euphrates to the farthest edge of the world.
72.9:
May even the desert peoples kneel before him.
May his enemies eat dust.
72.10:
Let the kings of Tarshish and the far coastlands bring tribute.
Let the kings of Sheba and Seba lay their gifts at his feet.
72.11:
Every king—bowing before him.
Every nation—his to serve.
72.12:
He rescues the poor when they cry out.
He reaches for the destitute—
the ones nobody else comes to help.
72.13:
He looks on the helpless with tenderness.
He saves the lives of those who have nothing.
72.14:
He redeems their lives from extortion and brutality.
In his eyes their blood is not cheap.
72.15:
May he live long!
May Sheba's gold be laid before him!
May people pray for him every day, all day,
and speak his praises without stopping.
72.16:
May grain grow in such abundance
that even the mountaintops are full of it, waving in the wind.
May the harvest be as rich as Lebanon's cedar forests.
May city-dwellers bloom like grass after rain.
72.17:
May his name last forever—
continuing as long as the sun exists.
May all peoples find their blessing in him,
and every nation call him blessed—
this is the promise made to Abraham, now placed on the royal son.
72.18:
Blessed be the LORD, Israel's God—
the one who alone does wonders.
72.19:
Blessed be his glorious name forever.
May the whole earth be filled with his glory.
Amen.
Amen.
72.20: Here end the prayers of David son of Jesse.

73.1:
A Psalm of Asaph.

Truly, God is good to Israel —
to all who are pure in heart.
73.2:
As for me — my feet nearly slipped;
I came within a step of going down.
73.3:
I was eaten up with envy of the arrogant —
I couldn't stop watching the wicked thrive.
73.4:
They die without a struggle;
their bodies are sleek and powerful to the end.
73.5:
They don't suffer the way ordinary people do;
they escape the troubles that wear everyone else down.
73.6:
So they wear their pride like a necklace
and drape themselves in violence like a robe.
73.7:
Their eyes bulge with excess;
the schemes of their hearts know no limit.
73.8:
They mock others and plot cruelty;
their boasting climbs all the way to heaven.
73.9:
They point their mouths straight at heaven
and strut their tongues across the whole earth.
73.10:
And so people keep flocking after them,
drinking up everything they offer.
73.11:
And they ask: 'Can God actually see this?
Does the Most High know anything at all?'
73.12:
Just look at them — the wicked —
coasting through life without a care,
piling up wealth all the while.
73.13:
What was the point of keeping my heart clean?
Of washing my hands in innocence —
if it all amounts to nothing?
73.14:
Every day I have been beaten down;
every morning brings another round of suffering.
73.15:
But if I had spoken these thoughts aloud,
I would have betrayed your people —
the whole community of your children.
73.16:
When I tried to work this out on my own,
it was nothing but confusion and pain —
73.17:
until I entered the sacred courts of God.
There I finally understood
what awaits them in the end.
73.18:
You have placed them on slippery ground,
poised to cast them headlong into ruin.
73.19:
In a single moment they collapse —
swept away, annihilated by sheer terror!
73.20:
They are like a dream that dissolves the moment you wake —
when you arise, O Lord,
their whole existence fades like smoke.
73.21:
There was a time when my heart was bitter with resentment,
when I felt stabbed through —
73.22: I was nothing but a brute, senseless as a beast in your presence.
73.23:
And yet — I am always with you;
you hold me by the right hand.
73.24:
You guide me with your wisdom,
and afterward you will take me up into glory.
73.25:
What do I have in heaven but you?
And on earth — there is nothing else I want.
73.26:
My body wastes away;
my heart gives out —
but God is the rock of my heart,
my portion and inheritance forever.
73.27:
Those who wander far from you will be destroyed;
you cut off everyone who breaks faith with you.
73.28:
But for me, drawing near to God is my greatest good —
I have made the Lord GOD my shelter,
so I can tell of all that he has done.

74.1:
A Maskil of Asaph.

O God, why have you abandoned us forever?
Why does your anger keep smoldering
against the flock you shepherd?
74.2:
Remember the people you claimed for yourself long ago —
the community you redeemed to be your inheritance.
Remember Mount Zion, where you chose to dwell.
74.3:
Come and see what is left —
desolation that stretches on without end.
See everything the enemy did to your sanctuary.
74.5:
They swung their axes like woodsmen in a thicket,
hacking through everything in sight.
74.6:
The carved woodwork — all of it —
they shattered with axes and hammers.
74.7:
They burned your sanctuary to the ground
and dropped the tabernacle of your name in the dust.
74.8:
'We will wipe them out completely,' they said —
and they burned down every place of worship in the land.
74.9:
We see no sign from you anywhere.
No prophet speaks.
None of us knows when this will end.
74.10:
How long, O God — how long?
Will the enemy go on mocking you?
Will they blaspheme your name forever?
74.11:
Why are you keeping your hand restrained —
your right hand tucked away?
Pull it out. Act.
74.12:
And yet — God has been my King since ancient days,
winning victories across the whole earth.
74.13:
You split the sea open by your power;
you crushed the heads of the sea-dragons on the waters.
74.14:
You broke every head of Leviathan
and fed his remains to the desert creatures.
74.15:
You split the earth to release springs;
you dried up rivers that had flowed without ceasing.
74.17:
You drew the boundaries of every land;
you shaped the seasons — summer and winter alike.
74.18:
Remember all of this, O LORD —
how the enemy has mocked you,
how this foolish people has reviled your name.
74.19:
Do not abandon your dove — your congregation —
to the pack of predators.
Do not forget your afflicted people forever.
74.20:
Look to your covenant!
Every dark corner of this land
is crammed full of brutal violence.
74.21:
Let not the oppressed be turned away in disgrace —
let the poor and needy praise your name instead.
74.22:
Rise up, O God — defend your own cause!
Remember how the fool mocks you day after day.
74.23:
Do not forget the roar of your enemies —
the clamor of those who rise against you
never stops rising.

75.1:
To the worship leader. 'Do Not Destroy.' A Psalm. A Song of Asaph.

We give you thanks, O God —
we give you thanks!
For your name is near;
we declare your wondrous works.
75.2:
When I choose the moment,
I will dispense justice without favor.
75.3:
When the whole earth trembles
and every person on it staggers —
I am the one who holds the pillars steady. Selah.
75.4:
I tell the arrogant: 'Stop strutting around!'
And to the wicked: 'Put that horn down.'
75.5:
Do not raise your horn against heaven;
do not speak with such stiff-necked pride.
75.6:
Power does not rise from east or west,
nor does it surge up out of the desert wilderness —
75.7:
it is God alone who judges,
bringing one low and raising another high.
75.8:
For in the LORD's hand there is a cup
brimming with wine, foaming and spiced —
he pours it out,
and all the wicked of the earth
shall drain it to the last bitter drop.
75.9:
But as for me — I will praise him forever;
I will sing to the God of Jacob.
75.10:
Every horn the wicked have raised —
I will cut them all down.
But the horns of the righteous
will rise.

76.2:
His tent is pitched in Salem;
his home is Zion.
76.3:
There — right there — he shattered the arrows in flight,
the shield, the sword,
every weapon of war. Selah.
76.4:
How glorious you are —
more majestic than mountains heaped with ancient spoil.
76.6:
A single word of rebuke from the God of Jacob —
and horse and chariot fell into death's deep sleep.
76.7:
You — you are the one to be feared.
Who could stand in your presence
when your anger blazes?
76.8:
You spoke judgment from heaven;
the earth heard and went utterly silent —
76.9:
when God rose to pronounce judgment
and rescue all the meek of the earth. Selah.
76.12:
He strips the proud breath from princes;
he is terrifying to every king on earth.

77.1:
To the choirmaster. To Jeduthun. A Psalm of Asaph.
I cried out to God with everything in me.
With all my voice I called—and he heard.
77.2:
On the day of my trouble I reached toward the Lord.
All night my hands stayed raised—I could not stop.
Deep within me refused every comfort.
77.3:
I thought of God—and groaned.
I kept turning it over until my spirit gave out.
Selah.
77.4:
You held my eyelids open—no sleep, no rest.
I was overwhelmed past the point of words.
77.5:
I reach back into the past—
those ancient years, those days of old.
77.6:
I remember the songs of the night.
I turn the question over in my heart.
My spirit searches—and keeps searching.
77.7:
Has the Lord abandoned us for good?
Will he never be favorable again?
77.8:
Is his steadfast love simply over—permanently?
Has his word run out, for every generation to come?
77.9:
Has God forgotten mercy?
Has anger slammed the door on his compassion?
Selah.
77.10:
Then I named it:
'This is my deepest wound—
that the right hand of the Most High once acted
and seems to act no more.'
But I will go back to what that hand once did.
77.11:
I will remember what the LORD has done.
Yes—I will remember your wonders from long ago.
77.12:
I will turn over everything you have done in my mind.
I will dwell on every act of your power.
77.13:
Your road runs through holiness, God—
through what is set apart and sacred.
What God is as great as you?
77.14:
You are the God of wonders.
You have made your power known to every nation.
77.15:
You stretched out your arm and bought back your people—
the children of Jacob and Joseph.
Selah.
77.16:
The waters saw you, God.
They saw you—and shuddered.
The depths convulsed.
77.17:
The clouds opened and emptied themselves.
The sky roared.
Your arrows blazed in every direction.
77.18:
Your thunder rolled through the churning sky.
Your lightning tore the world open with light.
The earth shook—all the way down.
77.19:
Your road cut straight through the sea—
your path ran through the great deep.
And no one could find your footprints.
77.20:
You led your people like a flock.
By the hand of Moses and Aaron
you brought them through.

78.1:
A teaching poem of Asaph.
Listen, my people—lean in close.
I have something to say, and I need you to hear it.
78.2:
I will open my mouth in a parable—
what I say will be layered, drawn from the ancient depths.
78.3:
What we have heard and verified—
what our fathers set in our hands—
78.4:
we will not keep hidden from our children.
We will pass it on to the next generation:
the LORD's glory,
his strength,
the wonders his hands have done.
78.5:
He built it into the structure of Israel—
a standing testimony in Jacob, a law among his covenant people—
and commanded our ancestors to hand it on.
78.6:
So that the coming generation would know—
even children not yet born—
and when they grew up, they would tell their own children.
78.7:
So they would learn to hope in God,
would not forget what he has done,
and would live by what he commanded.
78.8:
Not like their ancestors.
That generation was stubborn and rebellious—
they would not set their hearts toward God,
and their spirit never settled into faithfulness.
78.9:
Ephraim—equipped, armed, ready.
And yet they turned and ran
on the day of battle.
78.10:
They broke the covenant.
They refused to walk in God's way.
78.11:
They forgot.
All of it—the wonders right before their eyes.
78.12:
Right in front of their ancestors he performed wonders—
in Egypt,
in the fields around Zoan.
78.13:
He split the sea open and walked them through.
The water stood in walls on either side.
78.14:
All day, a cloud overhead to guide them.
All night, a pillar of fire.
78.15:
He cracked open rocks in the wilderness
and gave them water to drink—
streams welling up from the depths.
78.16:
He drew flowing streams from solid rock—
water running like rivers.
78.17:
And still they sinned—
more and more of it.
In the wilderness they provoked the Most High.
78.18:
They tested God—a deliberate choice, made in their hearts.
They demanded what they wanted to eat.
78.19:
They spoke against God.
'Can God manage a dinner table out here in the wilderness?' they said.
78.20:
'Yes, he hit a rock and water came rushing out—
creeks everywhere.
But bread? Can he do bread?
Meat for this whole crowd?'
78.21:
The LORD heard all of it—
and he was furious.
Fire broke out against Jacob.
Wrath rose up against Israel.
78.22:
They refused to believe in God.
They put no stock in his power to save them.
78.23:
He had already commanded the clouds.
He had already opened heaven's doors.
78.24:
He had rained manna down for them to eat—
grain from heaven itself.
78.25:
People ate what angels eat—
food sent down from above, as much as they could hold.
78.26:
He sent the east wind sweeping through the sky
and drove the south wind along with it.
78.27:
Meat fell from the sky like dust.
Birds came down as thick as sand at the seashore.
78.28:
It fell right in their camp—
around every tent.
78.29:
They ate. They were completely full.
He had given them everything they asked for.
78.30:
But they hadn't finished swallowing—
the food still in their mouths—
when their craving was still alive in them.
78.31:
God's wrath fell on them.
He killed the strongest ones—
the young men of Israel fell.
78.32:
And yet—they kept sinning.
All his wonders, and still they would not believe.
78.33:
So their days vanished into nothing.
Their years ran out in sudden panic.
78.34:
Every time he struck them, they went looking for him.
They turned around and earnestly sought God.
78.35:
They would remember: God is their rock.
The Most High is the one who buys them back.
78.36:
But it was flattery.
They used the right words
and meant none of them.
78.37:
Their hearts were somewhere else entirely.
They had no intention of keeping the covenant.
78.38:
But he—he was full of compassion.
He covered their guilt and did not destroy them.
Again and again he turned back his anger—
he would not let all his fury off the leash.
78.39:
He remembered what they were:
flesh.
A breath that goes out and never comes back.
78.40:
How many times—
how many times they pushed against him in the wilderness,
wounding him in the desert.
78.41:
Over and over they tested God.
They kept pushing against the Holy One of Israel—
pressing the bruise.
78.42:
They forgot what his hand had done.
They forgot the day he set them free from the enemy—
78.43:
all those signs he performed in Egypt,
those wonders in the fields of Zoan.
78.44:
He turned the river to blood.
Every stream—undrinkable.
78.45:
He sent clouds of flies that devoured them.
He sent frogs that swept through everything.
78.46:
Everything they had grown—
he handed to the locust and the caterpillar.
78.47:
Hail took the vines.
Frost killed the sycamore trees.
78.48:
He gave their cattle over to the hail—
their flocks to the lightning.
78.49:
He sent his burning anger like an army against them:
wrath, fury, anguish—
a force of destroying angels.
78.50:
He cleared the road for his anger—
nothing in the way.
He did not spare their very lives from death;
he handed them over to plague.
78.51:
He struck every firstborn in Egypt—
the best of their strength,
in every home across the land of Ham.
78.52:
But his own people he led out like sheep—
guided them through the wilderness like a flock.
78.53:
He kept them safe—they had nothing to fear.
But the sea swallowed their enemies whole.
78.54:
He brought them to the edge of his holy land—
the mountain that his right hand had won for them.
78.55:
He cleared the nations out ahead of them,
divided the land and gave it to Israel,
and settled each tribe in its place.
78.56:
And still they tested God.
Still they provoked the Most High.
Still they refused to keep what he had given them.
78.57:
They turned back.
Faithless—just like their ancestors.
They bent away like a bow with a warped limb,
flying wide of the mark.
78.58:
Their high places made him angry.
Their carved idols stirred his jealousy to the root.
78.59:
God heard it all.
He was furious.
He turned from Israel with revulsion.
78.60:
He walked away from the tabernacle at Shiloh—
the tent he had pitched among them.
78.61:
He gave his strength away to captivity.
His glory—into enemy hands.
78.62:
He gave his own people to the sword.
He turned in fury against the people he called his own.
78.63:
Fire consumed the young men.
The young women—no wedding songs were sung for them.
78.64:
Their priests fell by the sword.
And their widows—too shattered even to weep.
78.65:
Then the Lord woke up—
like someone roused from sleep,
like a warrior who had drunk deep and now rose, ready.
78.66:
He came at his enemies from behind
and humiliated them—
a shame that will not lift.
78.67:
He passed over the tent of Joseph.
Ephraim—he did not choose that tribe.
78.68:
He chose Judah instead.
He chose Mount Zion—the one he loved.
78.69:
He built his sanctuary high—
like the heights of heaven,
like the earth he put in place to last forever.
78.70:
He chose David—his servant.
He took him from the sheepfolds.
78.71:
From behind nursing ewes he brought him
to tend Jacob—his people—
and Israel—his inheritance.
78.72:
He shepherded them from an honest heart.
He guided them with hands that knew what they were doing.

79.2:
The corpses of your servants they flung out
as carrion for the birds of the sky;
the flesh of your faithful they left
for the beasts of the field.
79.3:
Their blood has been poured out like water
all around Jerusalem —
and there was no one left to bury them.
79.4:
We have become the taunt of our neighbors —
scorned and mocked
by everyone who surrounds us.
79.5:
How long, O LORD — how long?
Will your anger smolder on without end?
Will your burning jealousy never cool?
79.6:
Pour out your wrath on the nations who refuse to know you —
on the kingdoms that will not call on your name!
79.7:
For they have swallowed Jacob whole
and left his home a wasteland.
79.9:
Help us, O God who saves us —
act for the honor of your name!
Deliver us. Forgive our sins.
Your name is at stake.
79.10:
Why should the nations sneer: 'Where is their God?'
Let them watch with their own eyes
as you answer the blood of your servants
that cries out before you.
79.13:
Then we — your people,
the flock you shepherd —
will praise you forever.
From age to age we will tell the whole story
of all you have done.

80.1:
To the worship leader. To the tune 'Lilies of the Covenant.' A Psalm of Asaph.

Hear us, O Shepherd of Israel —
you who lead Joseph like a flock,
you who are enthroned above the cherubim:
shine forth!
80.2:
Before the tribes of Ephraim, Benjamin, and Manasseh —
stir up your power
and come to rescue us!
80.4:
O LORD God of hosts — how long
will you fume against the very prayers
your people bring before you?
80.5:
You have given us tears for bread
and measured out cups full of weeping for us to drink.
80.6:
You have made us an object of dispute among our neighbors;
our enemies laugh at what has become of us.
80.8:
You uprooted a vine from Egypt,
cleared the land by driving out the nations,
and planted it here.
80.10:
Its shade covered the mountains;
its branches grew like the great cedars of Lebanon.
80.11:
Its branches reached the sea to the west;
its shoots stretched east to the Euphrates.
80.12:
Why, then, have you torn down its walls?
Now every passerby stops and strips it bare.
80.13:
The wild boar out of the forest tears it apart;
the beasts of the field devour whatever remains.
80.16:
It has been burned; it has been hacked to the ground —
let those who did this perish
at a single word of rebuke from your face.
80.17:
Let your hand rest on the one at your right hand —
the son of man
you yourself have chosen and strengthened.
80.18:
Then we will never turn away from you again.
Give us new life,
and we will call on your name.

81.1:
To the worship leader. To the tune of the Gittith. A Psalm of Asaph.

Sing out to God, our strength —
shout for joy to the God of Jacob!
81.2:
Strike up the music!
Bring the tambourine,
the sweet-sounding lyre, and the harp.
81.3:
Sound the trumpet at the new moon —
at the full moon, on the appointed day,
the day of our great festival.
81.4:
This is what God has required of Israel —
a law laid down by the God of Jacob himself.
81.5:
He made it a covenant requirement for Joseph
when God went out over the land of Egypt.
Now I hear a voice I had not known before —
81.6:
'I lifted the crushing load from his shoulder;
I freed his hands from the slave's brick-basket.'
81.8:
'Hear me, my people — let me speak plainly:
O Israel, if you would only listen!'
81.9:
'No foreign god may have a place among you;
you must never bow to any god but me.'
81.10:
'I am the LORD your God —
the one who brought you out of Egypt.
Open your mouth as wide as you can;
I will fill it.'
81.11:
'But my people refused to hear my voice;
Israel wanted nothing to do with me.'
81.12:
'So I let them go —
I handed them over to the desires of their own stubborn hearts
to do exactly as they pleased.'
81.13:
'Oh, if only my people would listen!
If only Israel would walk in my ways!'
81.14:
'How swiftly I would have crushed their enemies
and turned my power against everyone who opposed them!'
81.15:
'Those who hate the LORD would have groveled before him,
and Israel's flourishing would have gone on forever.'

82.2:
'How long will you keep pronouncing corrupt verdicts,
showing favor to the wicked at every turn? Selah.'
82.3:
'Defend those who have nothing — the poor, the orphan;
see that justice is actually done for the afflicted and the needy.'
82.4:
'Rescue the poor and the needy;
pry them out of the wicked's grasp.'
82.5:
'They will not know. They will not understand.
They walk through the world in moral darkness —
and when justice fails at the top,
the very foundations of the earth give way.'
82.6:
'I myself declared it: you are gods —
every one of you a son of the Most High.'

83.1:
A Song. A Psalm of Asaph.

O God, do not stay silent!
Do not hold back. Do not be still, O God —
83.2:
for look — your enemies are roaring;
those who hate you have lifted their heads in open defiance.
83.3:
They are plotting in secret against your people,
consulting against the ones you hold as your own treasure.
83.4:
'Come,' they say, 'let us erase them from the map —
let the name Israel vanish from memory forever.'
83.5:
They have formed one united conspiracy;
they have made a pact against you —
83.6:
the clans of Edom and the Ishmaelites,
Moab and the Hagrites,
83.7:
Gebal, Ammon, and Amalek,
Philistia alongside the people of Tyre.
83.8:
Even Assyria has entered the alliance —
lending its military power to the cause of Lot's descendants. Selah.
83.9:
Deal with them as you dealt with Midian,
as you dealt with Sisera and Jabin at the river Kishon —
83.10:
who were annihilated at En-dor
and became nothing but dung rotting in the ground.
83.11:
Make their leaders like Oreb and Zeeb;
let all their commanders meet the end of Zebah and Zalmunna —
83.12:
those who boasted: 'We will seize God's own land
and claim it as ours.'
83.13:
O my God, make them like tumbleweeds —
like dry chaff the wind scatters
and never finds again.
83.14:
As wildfire sweeps through a forest,
as flame leaps to set whole mountains burning —
83.15:
so pursue them with your storm;
drive them into terror with your hurricane.
83.16:
Cover their faces with shame —
perhaps in their humiliation
they will finally seek your name, O LORD.
83.17:
Let them be shamed and shattered — forever;
let them be disgraced and destroyed.
83.18:
Let every nation learn at last
that you alone — Yahweh is your name —
are the Most High over all the earth.

84.1:
To the worship leader. To the tune of the Gittith. A Psalm for the sons of Korah.

How beloved is your dwelling place —
how beautiful!
O LORD of hosts!
84.2:
My whole being longs for the courts of the LORD —
I am faint with longing.
My heart, my very flesh, cries out
for the living God.
84.3:
Even the sparrow has found a home there,
and the swallow a nest where she can lay her young —
right there beside your altars,
O LORD of hosts, my King, my God!
84.4:
How blessed are those who live in your house —
they never stop praising you. Selah.
84.5:
Blessed is the person whose strength is in you —
the one who already has the pilgrim roads to Zion
written on their heart.
84.7:
They move from strength to strength,
until every one of them stands
before God in Zion.
84.8:
O LORD God of hosts — hear my prayer!
Give ear to me, O God of Jacob. Selah.
84.9:
Look on us, O God — our shield!
Turn your face toward your anointed king.
84.10:
One day in your courts outweighs a thousand anywhere else.
I would rather stand at the door of my God's house as a beggar
than make myself at home in the finest tent the wicked can offer.
84.11:
For the LORD God is sun and shield —
sun: the source of all light and life;
shield: our protection against all that would destroy us.
He lavishes favor and honor
and withholds not one good thing
from those who walk in integrity.
84.12:
O LORD of hosts —
blessed, truly blessed,
is everyone who puts their trust in you.

85.1:
A Psalm of the Sons of Korah. For the worship leader.

O LORD, you showed favor to your land —
you brought your people back,
you turned the fortunes of Jacob.
85.2:
You forgave the guilt of your people;
you buried all their sin out of sight. Selah.
85.3:
You took back every last measure of your wrath;
you turned away from the blazing heat of your anger.
85.4:
Do it again, O God who saves us —
turn us around and let your anger against us end.
85.5:
Will you stay angry with us forever?
Will this fury reach down through every generation yet to come?
85.6:
Will you not breathe new life into us —
so your people can burst into joy before you?
85.7:
Let us see your steadfast love, O LORD —
grant us your saving help.
85.8:
I will listen for what God the LORD is saying —
for he speaks peace to his people and to his faithful ones.
Only this: let them not drift back into foolishness.
85.10:
Steadfast love and faithfulness find each other;
righteousness and peace lean in and kiss.
85.11:
Faithfulness rises like a plant out of the ground;
righteousness bends down and gazes out from heaven.
85.12:
Yes — the LORD will give what is truly good,
and our land will pour out its abundance.
85.13:
Righteousness goes ahead of him like a herald,
marking out the way where his feet will walk.

86.2:
Guard my life — I am yours, set apart for you.
Save your servant who takes refuge in you,
O my God.
86.3:
Show me your favor, O Lord —
I have been calling to you all day without stopping.
86.4:
Fill your servant's heart with gladness —
it is to you, O Lord, that I raise my whole being.
86.5:
You, O Lord, are genuinely good —
quick to forgive,
overflowing with covenant love for everyone who calls to you.
86.6:
Listen to this prayer, O LORD —
pay close attention to my plea for mercy.
86.7:
On the day trouble overwhelms me, I call to you —
because you answer.
86.8:
None of the so-called gods compares to you, O Lord —
not one of them can do what you do.
86.10:
You are great and you do astonishing things —
you and no other are God.
86.11:
Show me the path you walk, O LORD —
I want to move through life in step with your truth.
Bring all the scattered pieces of my heart into one
so that I can truly honor your name.
86.12:
I will praise you with everything I have, O Lord my God;
I will keep lifting your name high — forever.
86.13:
Your covenant love toward me is immense —
you pulled me back from the pit of Sheol,
from the very lowest depths of death.
86.15:
But you, O Lord — you are a God of deep compassion,
warm and gracious,
slow to ignite,
overflowing with covenant love and unwavering faithfulness.
86.17:
Give me a visible sign of your goodness —
let those who hate me see it and be humiliated,
because you, O LORD, are the one who has helped me
and held me close.

87.1:
A Psalm. A Song of the Sons of Korah.

God has built his city on the holy mountains —
its foundations rest on sacred ground.
87.2:
The LORD loves the gates of Zion
more than any other place where Jacob pitches his tent.
87.3:
Glorious things have been said about you,
O city of God. Selah.
87.4:
Among those who know me, God declares:
'Rahab — Egypt — and Babylon I will count among my own;
Philistia, Tyre, even Ethiopia —
of each one I say: this one was born there.'
87.5:
Of Zion they will say: 'Every one of them was born here' —
and it is the Most High himself who will establish her.
87.6:
The LORD keeps the registry.
When he enrolls the nations he writes beside each name:
'Born in Zion.' Selah.
87.7:
Singers and dancers together cry out:
'Every spring that feeds our life is found in you.'

88.1:
A Song. A Psalm of the Sons of Korah. For the worship leader. According to Mahalath Leannoth. A Maskil of Heman the Ezrahite.

O LORD, God of my salvation —
I cry out to you day and night.
88.2:
Let my prayer reach you.
Bend your ear toward what I am crying out.
88.4:
I have been classified among those sinking into the pit —
written off like a man who has nothing left in him.
88.5:
Released into the company of the dead —
like soldiers abandoned in the grave
whom you hold in memory no longer,
cut off from everything your hand provides.
88.6:
You yourself have lowered me into the deepest pit —
into darkness,
into the black deeps below.
88.7:
The full weight of your wrath is crushing me;
you have broken over me, wave after wave. Selah.
88.8:
You have driven away everyone who knew me —
made me something they recoil from.
I am locked in with no way out.
88.10:
Do you work miracles for the dead?
Can those who have died rise to praise you? Selah.
88.11:
Does anyone in the grave speak of your love?
Do the inhabitants of Abaddon testify to your faithfulness?
88.12:
Can your astonishing works be known down in the dark?
Can your justice be spoken of in the land where nothing is remembered?
88.13:
But I — I keep crying to you, O LORD.
Every morning my prayer rises up to meet you.
88.14:
O LORD — why have you thrown me away?
Why do you keep hiding your face from me?
88.16:
The torrent of your blazing wrath has surged over me;
your assaults have cut me down.
88.17:
Like a flood they circle me all day long;
they converge on me from every side.
88.18:
You have taken from me every lover and every friend —
they are all gone.
Darkness alone remains.

89.1:
A teaching poem of Ethan the Ezrahite.
Of the LORD's steadfast love I will sing — forever.
With my own mouth I will declare your faithfulness
to every generation that comes after.
89.2:
I have said it:
your steadfast love is being built up to last forever.
You have fixed your faithfulness in the heavens themselves.
89.3:
'I have cut a covenant with the one I chose.
I have sworn my oath to David — my servant:'
89.4:
'Your line will stand — forever.
I will build your throne
for every generation to come.'
Selah.
89.5:
The heavens praise your wonders, LORD—
your faithfulness rings out
in the assembly of the holy ones.
89.6:
Who in all of heaven can compare to the LORD?
Among all the heavenly beings —
who is like him?
89.7:
He is held in awe in the council of the holy ones—
great, terrifying,
lifted above everything and everyone that circles him.
89.8:
LORD, God of the heavenly armies—
who is as mighty as you?
Your faithfulness is your very atmosphere.
89.9:
You hold the raging sea in check.
When the waves rise and rear up—
you still them.
89.10:
You crushed Rahab like a body laid out in defeat—
the chaos-dragon, the old enemy.
With the full strength of your arm
you scattered everyone who opposed you.
89.11:
The heavens are yours.
The earth is yours.
You laid the foundations of the world
and filled it with life.
89.12:
North and south — you made them all.
Tabor and Hermon sing for joy
at the sound of your name.
89.13:
Your arm is strength itself.
Your hand is strong.
Your right hand is lifted high.
89.14:
Righteousness and justice — these are what your throne rests on.
Steadfast love and truth
walk out ahead of you.
89.15:
How blessed are the people who know what it is
to shout for joy before you—
they live in the light of your face, LORD.
89.16:
All day long your name is their joy.
Your righteousness lifts them higher.
89.17:
You are the source of everything that makes them strong.
By your favor our horn is lifted high.
89.18:
Our shield belongs to the LORD.
The Holy One of Israel — he is our king.
89.19:
You spoke it as a vision—
words to those who followed you:
'I have set my strength on a warrior.
I have lifted up the one I chose from the people.'
89.20:
'I found David — my servant.
With my holy oil I anointed him,'
89.21:
'my hand to sustain him,
my arm to make him strong.'
89.22:
'No enemy will get the upper hand over him.
No man of wickedness will crush him.'
89.23:
'Before his eyes I will crush his enemies—
strike down every one who hates him.'
89.24:
'My faithfulness will be at his side.
My steadfast love will never leave him.
In my name his strength will rise.'
89.25:
'I will stretch his hand to the sea—
his right hand to the great rivers.'
89.26:
'And he will cry to me:
"You are my Father—
my God, the Rock of my salvation."'
89.27:
'I will make him my firstborn—
higher than any king who walks the earth.'
89.28:
'My steadfast love I will keep for him — always.
My covenant with him will hold.'
89.29:
'His children will endure — forever.
His throne — as long as the sky stands.'
89.30:
'If his children abandon my teaching
and refuse to walk in what I have laid down,'
89.31:
'if they violate my statutes
and set aside my commands,'
89.32:
'then I will deal with their rebellion—
a rod across the transgression, stripes for the sin.'
89.33:
'But my steadfast love I will not strip away.
My faithfulness I will not betray.'
89.34:
'I will not break the covenant.
I will not walk back a single word I have spoken.'
89.35:
'I have sworn it — by my own holiness, once for all.
I will not lie to David.'
89.36:
'His line will endure — forever.
His throne will stand as long as the sun burns before me.'
89.37:
'Like the moon, fixed in place forever—
a faithful witness in the sky above.'
Selah.
89.38:
But you—
you have cast him off.
You have been furious with your anointed one.
89.39:
You set aside the covenant with your servant.
You threw his crown into the dirt.
89.40:
You broke down every wall that protected him.
You turned his strongholds to rubble.
89.41:
Everyone passing by plunders him.
He has become a joke to those who once called him neighbor.
89.42:
You gave his enemies the upper hand.
You let everyone who hates him shout for joy.
89.43:
You turned his own sword dull.
You let him fall in battle
without holding him up.
89.44:
His splendor — you ended it.
His throne — you threw it to the ground.
89.45:
You cut short the years of his youth.
You dressed him in shame.
Selah.
89.46:
How long, LORD?
Will you hide yourself away forever?
How long will your wrath keep burning?
89.47:
Remember how short my time is—
how brief the span you gave us.
For what emptiness did you make them,
all the children of Adam?
89.48:
What man lives and escapes death?
Who can pull his very life
from the grip of the grave?
Selah.
89.49:
Lord — where is the steadfast love you showed before?
The love you swore to David in your faithfulness?
Where has it gone?
89.50:
Remember, Lord, how your servants are mocked.
How I carry it here, pressed against my chest—
the taunts of every people.
89.51:
With that same contempt your enemies have mocked, LORD—
mocked the very footsteps of your anointed one.
89.52:
Blessed be the LORD — forever.
Amen and Amen.

90.1:
A Prayer of Moses the man of God.
Lord — in every generation,
you have been where we live.
90.2:
Before the mountains were born—
before you shaped the earth and all that fills it—
from eternity to eternity,
you are God.
90.3:
You turn people back to dust
and say,
'Return, children of Adam.'
90.4:
A thousand years in your sight
are like yesterday, already gone—
like a single watch in the night.
90.5:
You sweep them away like a flood.
They are a dream—
like grass that springs up in the morning.
90.6:
In the morning it rises and blooms.
By evening it is cut down and withered.
90.7:
Your anger consumes us.
Your wrath leaves us undone.
90.8:
You have laid out our iniquities before you—
even our secret sins,
exposed in the light of your face.
90.9:
All our days pass away under your wrath.
We finish our years with a sigh—
a breath, and they are gone.
90.10:
Seventy years — that is what we get.
Eighty, if strength holds.
But even those years are burdened with toil and grief.
Then quickly, quickly — we fly away.
90.11:
Who really understands the power of your anger?
Who fears your wrath
the way it deserves to be feared?
90.12:
So teach us to count our days—
that we may bring home
a heart of wisdom.
90.13:
Return, LORD — how long?
Take pity on your servants.
90.14:
Fill us in the morning with your steadfast love.
Then we will rejoice—
and be glad through all our days.
90.15:
Make our gladness equal our grief—
joy that answers year for year
the time we spent in trouble.
90.16:
Show your work to your servants.
Let your glory be what their children see.
90.17:
Let the beauty of the LORD our God rest on us.
Establish the work of our hands—
yes, Lord — establish it.

91.1:
Whoever makes their home in the shelter of the Most High
will rest in the shadow of the Almighty.
91.2:
I say to the LORD:
'You are my refuge, my fortress —
my God. I trust you.'
91.3:
He will pull you free from the trap the hunter set—
and from the deadly plague.
91.4:
He spreads his feathers over you—
under his wings you find shelter.
His faithfulness is your shield,
your rampart that holds.
91.5:
You will not dread the terror that prowls the night,
nor the arrow loosed in broad day,
91.6:
nor the plague that moves through the darkness,
nor the destruction that strikes at full noon.
91.7:
A thousand may fall at your side,
ten thousand at your right hand—
but it will not touch you.
91.8:
You will only watch with your own eyes
and see what becomes of the wicked.
91.9:
Because you have made the LORD your refuge—
the Most High the place where you live—
91.10:
no evil will be allowed to reach you,
no plague will come near where you live.
91.11:
He will command his angels concerning you—
to guard you in every path you take.
91.12:
They will carry you in their arms
so your foot never strikes a stone.
91.13:
You will walk over the lion and the serpent—
trample the young lion,
trample the ancient snake.
91.14:
'Because he has clung to me in love,' says the LORD,
'I will deliver him.
I will lift him out of reach—
because he knows my name.'
91.15:
'When he calls — I will answer.
I will be with him in his trouble.
I will rescue him and cover him with honor.'
91.16:
'I will fill him with long life
and show him my salvation.'

92.1:
A Psalm. A Song for the Sabbath day.
How good it is to give thanks to the LORD—
to sing praises to your name, Most High,
92.2:
to declare your steadfast love each morning
and your faithfulness through the night,
92.3:
with a ten-stringed lute,
the rippling melody of the harp.
92.4:
You have made me glad, LORD,
by everything you have done.
I shout for joy
at the work of your hands.
92.5:
How great your works are, LORD!
How deep your designs—
beyond all measuring.
92.6:
The senseless person does not see it.
The fool never understands:
92.7:
the wicked may spring up like grass,
every evildoer may flourish—
but it is only so they can be destroyed forever.
92.8:
But you, LORD—
you are high above it all, forever.
92.9:
Look — your enemies, LORD.
Your enemies will perish.
All who do evil will be scattered.
92.10:
But you have raised my strength high—
like the horn of a wild ox.
You have anointed me with fresh oil.
92.11:
My own eyes have seen it—
what happens to those who hated me.
With my own ears I have heard the ruin
of those who rose against me.
92.12:
The righteous flourish like the palm tree—
they grow tall and strong
like a cedar of Lebanon.
92.13:
Planted in the house of the LORD—
they flourish in the courts of our God.
92.14:
Even in old age they bear fruit—
still vigorous, still green,
92.15:
still bearing witness:
the LORD is just.
He is my rock,
and there is no wrong in him.

93.1:
The LORD reigns!
He is clothed with majesty—
dressed in strength, girded with power.
The world stands firm,
unshakeable.
93.2:
Your throne was set from the beginning—
you yourself are from eternity.
93.3:
The rivers roar, LORD—
the rivers roar and raise their voices.
The great floods thunder their waves.
93.4:
Mightier than the roar of countless waters—
mightier than the crashing of the sea—
the LORD on high is mighty.
93.5:
Your decrees are utterly reliable, LORD.
Holiness adorns your house—
forevermore.

94.1:
LORD, God who avenges—
God of justice, rise up!
Shine forth!
94.2:
Rise up, Judge of the earth—
pay back the proud
what their pride has earned.
94.3:
How long, LORD?
How long will the wicked—
how long will the wicked keep celebrating?
94.4:
They pour out arrogant words.
All who do evil boast
without shame.
94.5:
Your people, LORD—
they crush them.
Your own inheritance — they grind it down.
94.6:
They murder widows and foreigners.
They kill the fatherless.
94.7:
And they say,
'The LORD doesn't see it.
The God of Jacob pays no attention.'
94.8:
Wake up, you most senseless among all the people.
You fools — when will wisdom finally reach you?
94.9:
The one who made the ear—
does he not hear?
The one who shaped the eye—
does he not see?
94.10:
The one who disciplines entire nations—
will he not correct?
The one who teaches all humanity—
94.11:
the LORD knows what people think.
He knows how empty it all is.
94.12:
Blessed is the one you discipline, LORD—
the one you teach from your own instruction,
94.13:
giving them rest from days of trouble
while the pit is being prepared for the wicked.
94.14:
The LORD will not abandon his people.
He will never forsake his inheritance.
94.15:
Justice will return to the upright.
All who hold a straight heart
will follow in its train.
94.16:
Who will rise up on my behalf against the wicked?
Who will stand with me
against those who do evil?
94.17:
If the LORD had not come to my aid—
in a moment I would have been in the silence of the grave.
94.18:
When I felt my foot slipping,
your steadfast love, LORD,
was what held me up.
94.19:
When my inner life was crowded with anxious thoughts,
your comfort was what brought me joy.
94.20:
Can a corrupt government count itself your partner—
one that enacts suffering
through the law itself?
94.21:
